intro.speaker: npc
intro.text: Oh hey! Welcome! I just put fresh coffee on. Want some? No? Okay, more for me.

intro_2.speaker: npc
intro_2.text: I'm Casey, Learning & Development. Three years of designing training programs that approximately 12% of the company voluntarily attends.

assess.speaker: npc
assess.text: So you're leading the AI thing. Need people trained up? Because I have some thoughts. Many thoughts.

choices.text: Morgan mentioned people are tired of changes.
We'll train people when we need to.

skill_gaps.speaker: npc
skill_gaps.text: Where do I start? Most of the team can use basic spreadsheets and our CRM. AI literacy? Data interpretation? Prompt engineering? We're starting from zero.

skill_gaps_2.speaker: npc
skill_gaps_2.text: But here's the thing — people want to learn. They're just scared of looking stupid. The trick is making it safe to not know things.

attendance.speaker: npc
attendance.text: Because training is optional, boring, and scheduled during people's busiest hours. I've been fighting for better time slots for two years.

attendance_2.speaker: npc
attendance_2.text: Also, the last 'AI training' was a vendor demo disguised as education. People felt tricked. Can't blame them.

morgan_connection.speaker: npc
morgan_connection.text: Morgan's right. Change fatigue is real. But you know what helps? When people feel competent. When they understand the tools they're asked to use. That's where I come in.

dismissive.speaker: npc
dismissive.text: Sure. 'Train when we need to.' That's how you end up with a $200K AI tool that everyone's afraid to touch. But hey, your call.

closing.speaker: npc
closing.text: Come talk to me when you're ready to invest in people. The tech is the easy part. Getting humans to use it? That's the real challenge.

check_in.speaker: npc
check_in.text: Hey! Got any updates on the training front? I've been sketching out curriculum ideas.

choices.text: Let's design a proper AI literacy program.
How's the team responding to the changes so far?

literacy_program.speaker: npc
literacy_program.text: Yes! Okay, I've been thinking about this. Short modules — 20 minutes max. Real examples from our data. Hands-on exercises, not just slides.

literacy_program_2.speaker: npc
literacy_program_2.text: And peer mentoring! Pair the early adopters with the skeptics. Nothing works better than seeing your colleague succeed.

mandatory_feedback.speaker: npc
mandatory_feedback.text: Mandatory... okay. Attendance will be 100% but engagement might be 30%. People show up, tune out, check a box. I've seen it a hundred times.

mandatory_feedback_2.speaker: npc
mandatory_feedback_2.text: But! If we make the content actually good? Relevant? Hands-on? We can turn compliance into enthusiasm. Let me redesign the curriculum.

team_response.speaker: npc
team_response.text: The engineers are excited — they always are. The account managers are cautious. The operations team is suspicious. Pretty standard distribution.

team_response_2.speaker: npc
team_response_2.text: The good news? Nobody's actively hostile yet. That's better than Falcon. The bar is low, but we're clearing it!
